attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of vomiting blood. He estimates he has vomited about 250 mL of bright red blood and continues to vomit small amounts. He has not sought medical advice for at least 15 years, when he was advised to reduce his alcohol intake. He currently drinks 15-20 standard drinks per day. His medical history is unknown and he takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell with visible jaundice. His pulse rate is 115/min, blood pressure 105/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. His abdomen is distended, and multiple spider naevi are visible on his upper trunk and face. Investigations reveal a haemoglobin of 85 g/L (130-180). An emergency endoscopy confirms active bleeding from oesophageal varices; banding is performed with no bleeding at the end of the procedure.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Transfusion of one unit of packed red blood cells
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with haematemesis due to oesophageal variceal bleeding, likely secondary to portal hypertension from alcohol-induced liver cirrhosis. After ensuring circulatory stability, the most important next step is the administration of intravenous terlipressin. Terlipressin causes splanchnic vasoconstriction, reducing portal collateral circulation and pressure, thereby helping to control bleeding. It should be commenced as a 2 mg IV bolus every 4 hours on presentation, then titrated down to 1 mg every 4 hours when bleeding is controlled. However, terlipressin can cause myocardial ischaemia, skin necrosis, and bowel ischaemia, so it may be contraindicated in patients with cardiovascular disease.

Transfusion of one unit of packed red blood cells is not appropriate. Over-transfusion could increase portal pressure and worsen bleeding. In patients with variceal bleeding, a restrictive transfusion strategy should be used to maintain haemoglobin above 70 g/L, which this patient is currently well above.

A 500 mL bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride is not indicated. The patient is not immediately haemodynamically unstable, and additional fluid support may increase portal pressure and exacerbate variceal bleeding.

Balloon tamponade may be used in the acute setting to achieve rapid haemostasis. However, this treatment is reserved for haemodynamically unstable patients and carries a risk of re-bleeding once the balloon is deflated. It also requires correct placement and is not used as a first-line treatment.

An esomeprazole infusion is recommended for patients with upper gastrointestinal bleeding when the cause is initially unknown. It suppresses stomach acid, which can contribute to haemostasis in peptic ulcer bleeding. However, in this case, the source of haemorrhage has been identified as variceal bleeding, making this option incorrect.

In addition to terlipressin, prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) should be administered to reduce the risk of infection, which is significantly increased in patients with liver cirrhosis. The overall management should include addressing the underlying liver disease and alcohol use disorder once the acute bleeding is controlled.
learningPoints: Terlipressin is an essential part of the management of a patient with active variceal haemorrhage.
Prophylactic antibiotics should be administered in patients with liver cirrhosis presenting with variceal bleeding.
Over-transfusion of blood products should be avoided in variceal bleeding to prevent increased portal pressure.
references: Simonetto DA, Liu M, Kamath PS. Portal hypertension and related complications: diagnosis and management. InMayo Clinic Proceedings 2019 Apr 1 (Vol. 94, No. 4, pp. 714-726). Elsevier.

Wells M, Chande N, Adams P, Beaton M, Levstik M, Boyce E, Mrkobrada M. Meta‐analysis: vasoactive medications for the management of acute variceal bleeds. Alimentary pharmacology & therapeutics. 2012 Jun;35(11):1267-78.
Grace ND. Diagnosis and treatment of gastrointestinal bleeding secondary to portal hypertension. American Journal of Gastroenterology (Springer Nature). 1997 Jul 1;92(7).
